Based on the search results, here are the key updates for Python 3.13 related to warnings and multiprocessing modules:

## Warnings Module

Python 3.13 comes with a new warnings.deprecated() decorator that can be used to emit warnings at runtime if a deprecated object/function/overload is used. Type checkers will also complain when an object is decorated with the warnings.deprecated() decorator.

## Multiprocessing Module

The default start method will change to a safer one on Linux, BSDs, and other non-macOS POSIX platforms where 'fork' is currently the default, though adding a runtime warning about this was deemed too disruptive as the majority of code is not expected to care. Use the get_context() or set_start_method() APIs to explicitly specify when your code requires 'fork'.

Additionally, multiprocessing.freeze_support() now checks for work on any "spawn" start method platform rather than only on Windows.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

results:
Based on the search results, here are the key updates for Python 3.13 related to warnings and multiprocessing modules:

## Warnings Module

Python 3.13 comes with a new warnings.deprecated() decorator that can be used to emit warnings at runtime if a deprecated object/function/overload is used. Type checkers will also complain when an object is decorated with the warnings.deprecated() decorator.

## Multiprocessing Module

The default start method will change to a safer one on Linux, BSDs, and other non-macOS POSIX platforms where 'fork' is currently the default, though adding a runtime warning about this was deemed too disruptive as the majority of code is not expected to care. Use the get_context() or set_start_method() APIs to explicitly specify when your code requires 'fork'.

Additionally, multiprocessing.freeze_support() now checks for work on any "spawn" start method platform rather than only on Windows.

content.thinking: The search gave me some general information, but I need to find the exact text from the official Python documentation. Let me fetch the official What's New In Python 3.13 documentation page to get the precise descriptions.

input.prompt: Find the sections for warnings and multiprocessing modules, provide exact text about what's new

content.content:
# What's New in Python 3.13 - warnings and multiprocessing Modules

## warnings Module

**PEP 702**: The new [`warnings.deprecated()`](../library/warnings.html#warnings.deprecated "warnings.deprecated") decorator provides a way to communicate deprecations to a [static type checker](../glossary.html#term-static-type-checker) and to warn on usage of deprecated classes and functions. A [`DeprecationWarning`](../library/exceptions.html#DeprecationWarning "DeprecationWarning") may also be emitted when a decorated function or class is used at runtime.

(Contributed by Jelle Zijlstra in [gh-104003](https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/104003).)

## multiprocessing Module

The default number of worker threads and processes is now selected using [`os.process_cpu_count()`](../library/os.html#os.process_cpu_count "os.process_cpu_count") instead of [`os.cpu_count()`](../library/os.html#os.cpu_count "os.cpu_count").

(Contributed by Victor Stinner in [gh-109649](https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/109649).)

---

**Key Difference**: The `multiprocessing` change represents an improvement in how worker processes are allocated by using a more appropriate CPU counting method that accounts for the calling thread's affinity, rather than the total system CPU count.

toolUseResult.result:
# What's New in Python 3.13 - warnings and multiprocessing Modules

## warnings Module

**PEP 702**: The new [`warnings.deprecated()`](../library/warnings.html#warnings.deprecated "warnings.deprecated") decorator provides a way to communicate deprecations to a [static type checker](../glossary.html#term-static-type-checker) and to warn on usage of deprecated classes and functions. A [`DeprecationWarning`](../library/exceptions.html#DeprecationWarning "DeprecationWarning") may also be emitted when a decorated function or class is used at runtime.

(Contributed by Jelle Zijlstra in [gh-104003](https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/104003).)

## multiprocessing Module

The default number of worker threads and processes is now selected using [`os.process_cpu_count()`](../library/os.html#os.process_cpu_count "os.process_cpu_count") instead of [`os.cpu_count()`](../library/os.html#os.cpu_count "os.cpu_count").

(Contributed by Victor Stinner in [gh-109649](https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/109649).)

---

**Key Difference**: The `multiprocessing` change represents an improvement in how worker processes are allocated by using a more appropriate CPU counting method that accounts for the calling thread's affinity, rather than the total system CPU count.
